(b) Decisions concerning clients of Lorenz, Alhadeff,

before the San Diego Unified Port oistrict?
3. what are your obligations with respect to knowing your
econornic interests and those of your spouse, and whether the
econornic interests will be affected by a decision of the district.
CONCLUSIONS
1. Where you receive income pursuant to a contract with the
firn for referring a client to the firm, onlv the client is the
source of this income, and not the f irrn. When you perfom
services for your client and pay a percentage of the fee to the
firn to cover overhead and use of the facilities, 
only the client
is the source of this incorne to you' when the firm refers clients
to you to perforrn the work and you receive a percentage. of the
totll 
fees biIled and collected by the f irrn, both the firrn and
clients are the source of this fu1I amount of income to you-
2. With respect to any clients of the firm frorn whom you
have not received a fee you must disqualify fron any decision
concerning such a client that will have a material' financial
effect on the firn. 
This is true even where the firn appears
before the district 
in a representative capacity representing the
client's the f irn.
3. You nust disqualify as to any governmental decision in
which you know or have reason to know you have a financial
intereit. 
As a general rule, an official 
rrhas reason to knowrt
that a decision will affect a source of income whenever a
reasonabLe person, under the same circumstances, would be like]y
to knohr the identity of the source of income and would be aware of
the decision's probable irnpact on the source.
FACTS
You are an appointed Conrnissioner of the San Diego Unified
Port District (the "district) . In addition you have a sole
proprietorship in a legal and consulting practice. 
Your 1a!,
practice operates through a consulting arrangement with the law
firrn of Lorer,z, Alhadeff, Lundin and oggel (the I'f irmrr) . Holrever,
you have no ownership or other interest in the firn.
Your consulting agreenent with the firll provides that your
1ega1 services are offered excLusively through the firm, and that
you represent both clients to the firm which are referred to you,
and clients which you bring to the firn. 
The contract provides
that your association with the firm is an independent contractual
relationship .


Our Flle No.  I-90-460
Page 3
You described the manner of compensation as follows:
(a) where you bring a client to the firn and legal services
are perforned by other attorneys in the firn, you are entitled to
a percentage of the professional fees billed and collected fron
the client, and the baLance belongs to the firn;
(b) where you perforn services for a client you have brought
to the firrn, the firn is entitled to certain percentage of the
total professional fees biIled and collected;
(c) where the firm refers a client to you, you receive a
percentage of the total fees bi11ed and collected for your
services and the firn retains the remainder.
You are seeking guidance with respect to two specific qroups
of clients of the firn. 
First you have asked about those clients
\rrho are sources of income to you (rtsource clientsrt). 
Second, you
have asked about all other clients of the firn from whon you have
received no part of their paynent for services (r'nonsource
clientsr') .
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits any public official 
from naking,
participating in naking, or otherwise usinq his official 
position
to influence a governmental decision in which the official 
has a
financial interest. 
A ttpublic officiaf i is defined in Section
82048 to include every member, officer, 
employee or public of-
ficial of a state or 1oca1 government agency. As a nember of the
san Diego Unified Port District, 
you are a rrpublic official 
as
defined in the Act. 
(Section a2o48. ) Thus, you may not use your
official 
position to make or participate in naking a governmental
decision in which you know or have reason to know you have a
financial interest.
1. Sources of Incone
Section 87103 specifies that a public official 
has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on the
official 
or a nember of his or her imnediate fanily or on:
Any source of income, other than gifts and
other than loans by a commercial lending institu-
tion in the regular course of business on terms
available to the public without regard to official
status, agtgregating two hundred fifty dollars
(S250) or rnore in value provided to, received by or
pronised to the public official within 12 nonths
prior to the tine when the decision j.s nade.
section 87103(c)


According to section 87]-03, any person or business that has
made any paynents to you is a source of income to you. If, in the
aggregate, the paynents totalled $250 or more in the past 12
rnonttri, the source is a potentially disqualifying financial
interest for the purposes of Section 87103.
In your Letter you describe three fact patterns by which you
receive income pursuant to your contract with the firn. 
The
factual distinctions under the fact patterns result in different
conclusions concerning the attribution of the incone you receive.
Under the first fact pattern, you are entitled to a percentage of
the professional fees bi1led and coLLected from a client where you
onty act as a "f inderrt in the transaction. In other l^'ords, you
recLive a fixed percentage of the fee for bringing a client to the
firm.
In In re carev ((L977) 3 FPPC ops. 99, copy enclosed) the
cornrnission concluded that !,rhere an individual had a contract wi'th
a business entity which provided that the individual had a
contractual righL to a portion of income received by the business
in a transaction, that lncome $ras treated as being direct from the
clients. 
Consequentty, under the first fact Pattern onlv the
clients are the source of this income, and not the firn.
Under the second fact pattern, you perforn services for your
client and you are entitled to the entire fee. However, you pay a
fixed percentage of the fee to the firm to cover overhead and use
of the facilities. 
Under this fact pattern it appears that only
the client is the source of this j'ncome to you.
In the third fact pattern, the firm refers clients to you to
perforrn the $rork. You receive a percentage of the total . fees
iriff"a and collected for your services and the firnr retains the
rernainder. Under these flcts it apPears that both the firm and
the clients are jointly responsible for the incorne you receive'
consequently, under the third fact pattern both the finn and
clients are the source of this fuII anount ;F-incorne to you.3/
where you deternine under any of the three arrangernents
described aLove that an individual or the f inn is a source of
income, the source nust be disclosed and you may not participate
in any decision that will have a reasonablv. foreseeable and
rnaterial financial elfeqt on the source of income.

This would include the firn

whether the financiaL consequences of a decision are reason-
ably foreseeable at the tirne a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case. An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it lrill occur. Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, 
it is not
reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC ops. 198,
copy enclosed. )
In addition, the foreseeable effect on your source of income
must also be naterial to require disqualification. 
The Connission
has adopted differinq gnridelines to deternine whether an effect is
material, depending on the specific circumstances of each deci-
sion. For example, where a source of income is directly before
the district, 
as an applicant or the subject of the decision,
Regulation 18702.1(a) (copy enclosed) provides that the effect of
the decision on a source of income is deerned material and
disqualification is required.'/ 
(combs Advice Letter, No.
A-89-L77 , copy enclosed. )
Where the source of income is not directly before the
district, 
but may be indirectly affected, Regulations 18702.2 and
la7o2.6 (copies enclosed) app1y. whether the indirect effect on a
business entity, such as the firn, is rnaterial dePends on the
financial size of the business entity. 
Regulation L8702.2(g)
provides that for a relatively small business entity, the indirect
effect of a decision is material where:
(1) The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year
of $10,OOO or more; or
(2) The decision !ril1 result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional, expenses or
reducing or elininating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or nore; or
(3) The decision hrill result in the increase
or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities
of $10,000 or nore.
4/ 
A source of income is directly before the district when the
source initiates the proceeding by filing an application, claim,
appeal, or sirnilar request, or is a narned party in, or the subiect
of, the proceeding. A person or business entity is the subject of
a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, rene$raI,
approval, denial or revocation of any 1i,cense, permit, or other
entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person or business
entity. (Regulation La702.1(b). )


Our Fl■e No.  I-90-460
Page 6
Fina11y, rrhere a source of income is a person and not
directly before the district, 
Regulation La702.6 provides:
The effect of a decision is ruaterial as to an
individual who is a source of incone or gifts to an
official 
if any of the follorrring aPplies:
(a) The decision will affect the
individual's income, investrnents, or other
tangible or intangible assets or liabilities
(other than reat property) by $1,000 or more,'
or
(b) ?he decision will affect the
individual's real property interest in a nan-
ner that is consi-dered rnaterial under Section
LBl02.3 or section La7 02 .4 .5 |
However, even if you determine that you have a financial
interest that wiLt be financially affected by the decision, you
nay sti1l participate if the effect on your interests is not
distinguishable fron the effect on the public Aenerally. The
Ipublictr consists of the entire jurisdiction of the agency in
question. (In re o$ren (1976) 2 FPPC ops. 77, copY enclosed.) For
the trpublic generallytr exception to apply, the decision must af-
fect your source of incorne in substantially the sarne manner as it
will affect a significant segTment of the district's 
jurisdiction.
(Regarlation 18703, copy enclosed.) since the exception is
necessarily fact dependent, we can only provide this general
outl ine .
2. Nonsource clients of the Firm
With respect to any clients of the firn not covered above,
you are obligated to disqualify from any district 
decision
-oncerning such a client that will have a rnaterial financial
effect on the firn. 
Thus, the effect of a decision on a nonsource
client of the firm would only be considered to the extent that the
effect on that client wilt result in an increase or decrease in
the qross revenues, the value of the assets or liabilities, 
or
expenses of the firm. 
Effects on nonsource clients of the firn
which will not affect the firm itself do not result in a conflict
of interest.
5/  Regu■ations ■8702.3 and
infoこ.llation.
la7o2.4 have been enclosed for your

Page 7
I-90-460
Moreover, hrhere the firm appears before the district 
in a
representative capacity representing nonsource clients, the firro
is not considered to be appearing directly before the district
provided the firrn is acting only as,an agent of client and has no
economic interest in the decision. o/ (ciII Advice Letter, No. A-
85-252, copy enclosed.) 
Disqual ification would be required where
the indirect financial effect on the firn will be material. 
For
example, where the clients payment to the firm for representation
was somehou contingent on the district's 
approval of the client's
application, then the district's 
decision could naterially
financially affect the firm to the extent set forth in Regulation
ta7 02 .2 .
3. The rrKnows or Has Reason to Knowrr Standard
An official 
knows that he or she has a financial interest in
a decision if the official 
actually knows that it is reasonably
foreseeable that a decision will materially affect a source of
income. As a general rule, an.official 
rrhas reason to know'r that
a decision will affect a source of incorne lrhenever a reasonable
person, under the same circurnstances, would be likely to know the
identity of the source of income and would be aware of the
decision's probable irnpact on the source. (Price Advice Letter,
No. A-85-155, copy enclosed. )
cenerally, officiats are presumed to know who have been
sources of incone to then. consequently, you would have reason to
know of a conflict of interest where the districtts 
materials
include the name of a source of income. Further, if a client owns
property near property subject to a dj-strict decision, and this
own-rsnip is known to you or otherwise weII publicized, you $rould
be expected to be aware of the potential conflict of interest-
In our letter to James Christiansen (Advice Letter, No. I-87-
019, copy enctosed) the requestor suqgested a uethod to avoid
accidentll participation in decisions in hlhich a source of income
to the requestor or the requestor's spouse night be involved. 
we
advised that the nethod would be helpful to the official 
in
conplying rrith the conflict-of-interest 
provisions of the Act-
Plelse note that the christiansen Advice Letter dealt with a
spouses partial interest in a partnership. Under your facts the
threshold for disclosure and disqualification is $250.

Iist of those applicants who are expected to appear

prior to the neeting. Her husband wouLd have his

ctients that were reported to her as exceeding the

Your proposed rnethod of deterrnining which
clients could create potential conflicts of
interest is basically a good approach. we have a
few comments, however. First, as we discussed
above, city council decisions rnay directly or
indirectly affect rnore than the apPlicants. For
example, if a decisi.on s/ere to affect real property
adjacent to property owned by one of Ms.
christensen's husband's cfients, and the effect on
the client's real property vrould be foreseeable,
material, and distinguishable fron the effect on
the public general]y, then Ms. Christensen rrould be
required to disqualify herself fron participating
in the decision. Therefore, we suggest that the
city staff provide Ms. Christensen with a
description of the subject matter of the decisions
pending before the city council, in addition to
providing a list of the applicants.
***
As you discussed in your letter,
t[s. christensen is required to disqualify herself
frorn participating in a decision if he knows or has
reason to know of her financial interest in the
decision. 
(Section 87100.) The approach you have
suggested would be evidence of Ms. Christensen's
good faith effort to conply with the
disqualification requirements of the Act. (Footnote
ornitted. ) Nevertheless, Ms. christensen should be
inforned that her responsibility for disclosure and
disqualification cannot be transferred to a staff
person in her husband's firm. 
If she becomes aware
of her husband's clients through other means, such
as during a social function or during conversations
with her husband, she rnay also know or have reason
to know of her financial interest in a particular
decision.
of course, whether you know or have reason to know is
necessarily a factual question and nust be deternined on a case-
by-case basis.


Our Fl■e No.  I-90-460
Page 9
I trust this letter has addressed your concerns. should you
have any further guestions regarding this natter or a specific
decision that you woul-d like advice on, please feel free to
contact ne at (916) 322-59oL.
Sincerely,
Scott HaIlabrin
Acting General counsel
John w. wallace
Counsel, Legal Division

whether the financiaL consequences of a decision are reason-
ably foreseeable at the tirne a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case. An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it lrill occur. Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, 
it is not
reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC ops. 198,
copy enclosed. )
In addition, the foreseeable effect on your source of income
